From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Subculture in England

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Old_Mods_photo.jpg/330px-Old_Mods_photo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Old_Mods_photo.jpg)

Two mid-1960s mods on a customised Lambretta [scooter](/wiki/Scooter_(motorcycle) "Scooter (motorcycle)")

**Mod**, from the word *modernist*, is a [subculture](/wiki/Subculture "Subculture") that began in late 1950s London and spread throughout Great Britain, eventually influencing fashions and trends in other countries.[[1]](#cite_note-life-1) It continues today on a smaller scale. Focused on music and fashion, the subculture has its roots in a small group of stylish London-based young men and women in the late 1950s who were termed *modernists* because they listened to [modern jazz](/wiki/Modern_jazz "Modern jazz").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Elements of the mod subculture include fashion (often tailor-made suits) and music (including [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music"), [rhythm and blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues") and [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska"), but mainly [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz")). They rode motor scooters, usually [Lambrettas](/wiki/Lambretta "Lambretta") or [Vespas](/wiki/Vespa "Vespa"). In the mid-1960s, members of the subculture listened to [pop](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music") groups with [rhythm and blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues") (R&B) influences, such as [the Who](/wiki/The_Who "The Who") and [Small Faces](/wiki/Small_Faces "Small Faces"). The original mod scene was associated with [amphetamine](/wiki/Amphetamine "Amphetamine")-fuelled all-night jazz dancing at clubs.[[3]](#cite_note-medicine-3)

During the early to mid-1960s, as the mod movement grew and spread throughout Britain, certain elements of the mod scene became engaged in well-publicised [clashes](/wiki/Riot "Riot") with members of a rival subculture, the [rockers](/wiki/Rocker_(subculture) "Rocker (subculture)").[[4]](#cite_note-Brawl-4) The conflict between [mods and rockers](/wiki/Mods_and_rockers "Mods and rockers") led sociologist [Stanley Cohen](/wiki/Stanley_Cohen_(sociologist) "Stanley Cohen (sociologist)") to use the term "[moral panic](/wiki/Moral_panic "Moral panic")" in his study about the two [youth subcultures](/wiki/Youth_subculture "Youth subculture"),[[5]](#cite_note-Moral_Panics-5) in which he examined media coverage of the mod and rocker riots in the 1960s.[[6]](#cite_note-British_Film_Commission_BFC-6)

By 1965, conflicts between mods and rockers began to subside and mods began to fade out with the dandy subculture taking over with increasingly gravitating towards [pop art](/wiki/Pop_art "Pop art") and [psychedelia](/wiki/Psychedelic_rock "Psychedelic rock"). London became synonymous with fashion, music, and pop culture in those years, a period often referred to as "[Swinging London](/wiki/Swinging_Sixties "Swinging Sixties")". During that time, mod influenced fashions spread to other countries. Mod was then viewed less as an isolated subculture, but as emblematic of the larger youth culture of the era. As mod became more cosmopolitan during the "Swinging London" period, some working-class "street mods" splintered off, forming other groups such as the [skinheads](/wiki/Skinhead "Skinhead").

By the early 1970s, mod had long faded out, as did psychedelia of the dandy culture with popularity, with [hard rock](/wiki/Hard_rock "Hard rock") and [glam rock](/wiki/Glam_rock "Glam rock") styles taking over. In the late 1970s, there was a [mod revival](/wiki/Mod_revival "Mod revival") in Britain, which attempted to replicate the "scooter" period look and styles of the early to mid-1960s. It was followed by a similar mod revival in North America in the early 1980s, particularly in [southern California](/wiki/Southern_California "Southern California").[[7]](#cite_note-disjointed-7)[[8]](#cite_note-socal-8)

## Etymology and usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mod_(subculture)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology and usage")]

The term *mod* derives from *modernist*, a term used in the 1950s to describe modern jazz musicians and fans.[[9]](#cite_note-9) That usage contrasted with the term *trad*, which described [traditional jazz](/wiki/Trad_jazz "Trad jazz") players and fans. The 1959 novel *[Absolute Beginners](/wiki/Absolute_Beginners_(novel) "Absolute Beginners (novel)")* describes modernists as young modern jazz fans who dress in sharp modern [Italian clothes](/wiki/Italian_fashion "Italian fashion"). The novel may be one of the earliest examples of the term being written to describe young British style-conscious modern jazz fans. That use of the word *modernist* should not be confused with *[modernism](/wiki/Modernism "Modernism")* in the context of literature, art, design and architecture. From the mid-to-late 1960s onwards, the mass media often used the term *mod* in a wider sense, to describe anything that was believed to be popular, fashionable or [modern](/wiki/Modern_era "Modern era").

Paul Jobling and David Crowley argued that the definition of *mod* could be difficult to pin down because, throughout the subculture's original era, it was "prone to continuous reinvention".[[10]](#cite_note-Graphic_Design_1800._Page_213-10) They claim that, since the mod scene was so pluralist, the word *mod* was an umbrella term that covered several distinct sub-scenes. Terry Rawlings argued that mods were difficult to define because the subculture started out as a "mysterious semi-secret world", which the Who's manager [Peter Meaden](/wiki/Peter_Meaden "Peter Meaden") summarised as "clean living under difficult circumstances".[[11]](#cite_note-autogenerated5-11)

## 1958–1970: Original movement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mod_(subculture)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1958–1970: Original movement")]

[George Melly](/wiki/George_Melly "George Melly") wrote that mods were initially a small group of clothes-focused young English [working class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class") men, insisting on clothes and shoes tailored to their style, who emerged during the modern jazz boom of the late 1950s.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Early mods watched French and Italian [art films](/wiki/Art_film "Art film") and read Italian magazines to look for style ideas.[[11]](#cite_note-autogenerated5-11) They usually held semi-skilled manual jobs or low grade [white-collar](/wiki/White-collar_worker "White-collar worker") positions such as a clerk, messenger or office boy. According to [Dick Hebdige](/wiki/Dick_Hebdige "Dick Hebdige"), mods created a parody of the consumer society that they lived in.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Early 1960s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mod_(subculture)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early 1960s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Quadrophenia_exhibit_2.jpg/250px-Quadrophenia_exhibit_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Quadrophenia_exhibit_2.jpg)

*[Quadrophenia](/wiki/Quadrophenia_(film) "Quadrophenia (film)")* exhibit at the Cotswold Motor Museum in Bourton-on-the-Water in 2007

According to Hebdige, by around 1963, the mod subculture had gradually accumulated the identifying symbols that later came to be associated with the scene, such as scooters, amphetamine pills and R&B music.[[14]](#cite_note-DickHebdige-14) While clothes were still important at that time, they could be ready-made. Dick Hebdige wrote the term *mod* covered a number of styles including the emergence of [Swinging London](/wiki/Swinging_London "Swinging London"), though to him it defined Melly's working class clothes-conscious teenagers living in London and south England in the early to mid-1960s.[[14]](#cite_note-DickHebdige-14)

Mary Anne Long argued that "first hand accounts and contemporary theorists point to the Jewish upper-working or middle-class of [London's East End](/wiki/East_End_of_London "East End of London") and suburbs."[[15]](#cite_note-nh-scooters.com-15) [Simon Frith](/wiki/Simon_Frith "Simon Frith") asserted that the mod subculture had its roots in the 1950s [beatnik](/wiki/Beatnik "Beatnik") coffee bar culture, which catered to art school students in the radical [Bohemian](/wiki/Bohemianism "Bohemianism") scene in London.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Steve Sparks, whose claim is to be one of the original mods, agrees that before mod became commercialised, it was essentially an extension of the [beatnik](/wiki/Beatnik "Beatnik") culture: "It comes from 'modernist', it was to do with modern jazz and to do with [Sartre](/wiki/Jean-Paul_Sartre "Jean-Paul Sartre")" and [existentialism](/wiki/Existentialism "Existentialism").[[15]](#cite_note-nh-scooters.com-15) Sparks argued that "Mod has been much misunderstood ... as this working-class, scooter-riding precursor of skinheads."

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Small_Faces_1965.JPG/250px-Small_Faces_1965.JPG)](/wiki/File:Small_Faces_1965.JPG)

[Small Faces](/wiki/Small_Faces "Small Faces") in 1965

Coffee bars were attractive to British youth because, in contrast to typical [pubs](/wiki/Public_house "Public house"), which closed at about 11 pm, they were open until the early hours of the morning. Coffee bars had [jukeboxes](/wiki/Jukebox "Jukebox") which, in some cases, reserved space in the machines for the customers' own records. In the late 1950s, coffee bars were associated with jazz and rock 'n' roll, but in the early 1960s, they began playing more R&B music alongside. Frith noted that although coffee bars were originally aimed at middle-class art school students, they began to facilitate an intermixing of youth from different backgrounds and classes.[[17]](#cite_note-17) At those venues, which Frith called the "first sign of the youth movement"[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*], young people met collectors of R&B and blues records.

As the mod subculture grew in London during the early-to-mid-1960s, [tensions arose](/wiki/Mods_and_rockers "Mods and rockers") between the mods, often riding highly decorated motor scooters, and their main rivals, the [rockers](/wiki/Rocker_(subculture) "Rocker (subculture)"), a British subculture who favoured [rockabilly](/wiki/Rockabilly "Rockabilly"), early [rock'n'roll](/wiki/Rock%27n%27roll "Rock'n'roll"), motorcycles and leather jackets, and considered the mods effeminate because of their interest in fashion.[[18]](#cite_note-Covach,_John_2012-18) There were some violent clashes between the two groups.[[18]](#cite_note-Covach,_John_2012-18) This period was later immortalised by songwriter [Pete Townshend](/wiki/Pete_Townshend "Pete Townshend"), in the Who's 1973 concept album, *[Quadrophenia](/wiki/Quadrophenia "Quadrophenia")*.[[19]](#cite_note-sleeve-19) After 1964, clashes between the two groups largely subsided, as mod expanded and came to be accepted by the youth generation throughout the UK as a symbol of all that was new.[[20]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-20)[[21]](#cite_note-telegraph.co.uk-21) During that time, London became a mecca for [pop](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music") music, with popular bands such as [The Who](/wiki/The_Who "The Who") and [Small Faces](/wiki/Small_Faces "Small Faces") appealing to a largely mod audience,[[22]](#cite_note-Bogdanov2002Mod-22) as well as the preponderance of hip fashions, in a period often referred to as [Swinging London](/wiki/Swinging_Sixties "Swinging Sixties").

### Mid-late 1960s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mod_(subculture)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Mid-late 1960s")]

#### Swinging London

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mod_(subculture)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Swinging London")]

See also: [Swinging London](/wiki/Swinging_London "Swinging London") and [peacock revolution](/wiki/Peacock_revolution "Peacock revolution")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Londons_Carnaby_Street%2C_1966.jpg/250px-Londons_Carnaby_Street%2C_1966.jpg)](/wiki/File:Londons_Carnaby_Street,_1966.jpg)

[Carnaby Street](/wiki/Carnaby_Street "Carnaby Street") in "Swinging London" c. 1966

As numerous British pop bands of the mid-1960s began to adopt a mod look and following, this was due to members of some of these bands originally being mods,[[22]](#cite_note-Bogdanov2002Mod-22) the scope of the subculture grew beyond its original confines, and the focus began to change. By 1966, mod had begun to fade out and the dandy subculture increased in popularity. Proletarian aspects of the dandy scene in London had waned as fashion and pop-culture elements continued to grow, not only in England, but elsewhere.[[1]](#cite_note-life-1)

This period, was typified by [pop art](/wiki/Pop_art "Pop art"), [Carnaby Street](/wiki/Carnaby_Street "Carnaby Street") boutiques, live music, and discothèques. Many associate this era with fashion model [Twiggy](/wiki/Twiggy "Twiggy"), [miniskirts](/wiki/Mini_skirt "Mini skirt"), and bold geometrical patterns on brightly coloured clothes. During these years, it exerted a considerable influence on the worldwide spread of mod,[[1]](#cite_note-life-1) such as being portrayed in Italian cinema in 1966 with [Alberto Sordi](/wiki/Alberto_Sordi "Alberto Sordi")'s *[Fumo di Londra](/wiki/Fumo_di_Londra "Fumo di Londra")*, and [Michelangelo Antonioni](/wiki/Michelangelo_Antonioni "Michelangelo Antonioni")'s *[Blowup](/wiki/Blowup "Blowup"),*[[23]](#cite_note-23)

#### United States and elsewhere

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mod_(subculture)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: United States and elsewhere")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/1960s_fashions_%281709303069%29.jpg/250px-1960s_fashions_%281709303069%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:1960s_fashions_(1709303069).jpg)

Woman wearing a [miniskirt](/wiki/Miniskirt "Miniskirt"), 1966

As the mod lifestyle was going through decline in England, its influence became all the rage in the United States and around the world, as many young people adopted its look.[[1]](#cite_note-life-1) However, the worldwide experience differed from that of the early scene in London in that it was based mainly on pop culture and influenced by British modern jazz and pop musicians. Mod came to be considered part of general youth culture and style rather than as a narrow subculture.[[20]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-20)[[21]](#cite_note-telegraph.co.uk-21)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

American musicians, in the aftermath of the [British Invasion](/wiki/British_Invasion "British Invasion"), adopted the look of the dandy which was popular in London, with [peacock revolution](/wiki/Peacock_revolution "Peacock revolution") clothes, longer hairstyles, and [Beatle boots](/wiki/Beatle_boots "Beatle boots").[[25]](#cite_note-25) The [exploitation](/wiki/Exploitation_film "Exploitation film") documentary [*Mondo Mod*](/wiki/Mondo_film "Mondo film") provides a glimpse into mod and dandy influence on the Sunset Strip and West Hollywood scene of late 1966.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Mod increasingly became associated with [psychedelic rock](/wiki/Psychedelic_rock "Psychedelic rock") and the early [hippie](/wiki/Hippie "Hippie") movement and, by 1967, more exotic looks had come into vogue, such as [Nehru jackets](/wiki/Nehru_jacket "Nehru jacket") and [love beads](/wiki/Love_beads "Love beads").[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-fashion-history.lovetoknow.com-29) Its trappings were reflected on popular American TV shows such as [*Laugh-In*](/wiki/Rowan_%26_Martin%27s_Laugh-In "Rowan & Martin's Laugh-In") and [*The Mod Squad*](/wiki/Mod_Squad "Mod Squad").[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-The_Mod_Squad_Iconic_counter-culture_Show-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)

#### Commercialisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mod_(subculture)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Commercialisation")]

[Dick Hebdige](/wiki/Dick_Hebdige "Dick Hebdige") argued that the subculture lost its vitality when it became commercialised and stylised to the point that mod clothing styles were being created "from above" by clothing companies and by TV shows like *[Ready Steady Go!](/wiki/Ready_Steady_Go! "Ready Steady Go!")*, rather than being developed by young people customising their clothes and combining different fashions.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

## 1970–present: Later developments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mod_(subculture)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1970–present: Later developments")]

Mod and psychedelia dissipated after 1970, as tastes began to favor a more casual look,[[35]](#cite_note-35) along with a decreased interest in nightlife. Bands such as the Who and Small Faces began to change and, by the early 1970s, moved away from mod to a more [hard rock](/wiki/Hard_rock "Hard rock") style.[[36]](#cite_note-Pitchfork_Staff_2004-36)[[37]](#cite_note-Allmusic-37) Other mods such as [David Bowie](/wiki/David_Bowie "David Bowie") and [Marc Bolan](/wiki/Marc_Bolan "Marc Bolan") (of [T. Rex](/wiki/T._Rex_(band) "T. Rex (band)")) adopted a [glam rock](/wiki/Glam_rock "Glam rock") style.[[38]](#cite_note-38) Additionally, the original mods of the late 1950s and early 1960s were coming to the age of marriage and child-rearing, which meant many of them no longer had the time or money for their youthful pastimes of club-going, record-shopping, and buying clothes.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

### Offshoots

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mod_(subculture)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Offshoots")]

See also: [Skinhead](/wiki/Skinhead "Skinhead") and [Scooterboy](/wiki/Scooterboy "Scooterboy")

Some street-oriented mods, usually of lesser means, sometimes referred to as *hard mods,* remained active well into the late 1960s, but tended to become increasingly detached from the Swinging London scene and the burgeoning hippie movement.[[40]](#cite_note-skinheads-40)[[41]](#cite_note-Mod_music-41) By 1967, they considered most of the people in the Swinging London scene to be "soft mods" or "peacock mods", as styles there became increasingly extravagant, often featuring highly ruffled, [brocade](/wiki/Brocade "Brocade")d, or laced fabrics in [Day-Glo](/wiki/Day-Glo "Day-Glo") colours.[[29]](#cite_note-fashion-history.lovetoknow.com-29)[[40]](#cite_note-skinheads-40)[[41]](#cite_note-Mod_music-41)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Wearethemods.jpg/250px-Wearethemods.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wearethemods.jpg)

Mod graffiti in Italy from 2007

Many of the hard mods lived in the same economically depressed areas of [South London](/wiki/South_London "South London") as [West Indian](/wiki/British_African-Caribbean_people "British African-Caribbean people") immigrants, so these mods favoured a different kind of attire, that emulated the [rude boy](/wiki/Rude_boy "Rude boy") look of [Trilby](/wiki/Trilby "Trilby") hats and too-short trousers.[[42]](#cite_note-Hebdige,_Dick_2000-42) These Mods listened to Jamaican [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") and mingled with black rude boys at West Indian nightclubs like Ram Jam, A-Train and Sloopy's.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45) Hebdige claimed that the hard mods were drawn to black culture and ska music in part because the educated, middle-class hippie movement's drug-orientated and intellectual music did not have any relevance for them.[[46]](#cite_note-46) He argued that the hard mods were attracted to ska because it was a secret, underground, non-commercialised music that was disseminated through informal channels such as house parties and clubs.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

By the end of the 1960s, the hard mods had become known as [skinheads](/wiki/Skinhead "Skinhead"),[[48]](#cite_note-skinheads_2-48) who, in their early days, would be known for the same love of [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music"), [rocksteady](/wiki/Rocksteady "Rocksteady") and early [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae").[[49]](#cite_note-BBC_Style-49)[[50]](#cite_note-Feldman-50)[[51]](#cite_note-Waters-51) Because of their fascination with black culture, the early skinheads were, except in isolated situations, largely devoid of the overt racism and fascism that would later become associated with whole wings of the movement in the mid to late 1970s.[[52]](#cite_note-52) The early skinheads retained basic elements of mod fashion—such as [Fred Perry](/wiki/Fred_Perry "Fred Perry") and [Ben Sherman](/wiki/Ben_Sherman "Ben Sherman") shirts, Sta-Prest trousers and [Levi's](/wiki/Levi%27s "Levi's") jeans—but mixed them with working class-orientated accessories such as [braces](/wiki/Suspenders "Suspenders") and [Dr. Martens](/wiki/Dr._Martens "Dr. Martens") work boots. Hebdige claimed that as early as the Margate and Brighton brawls between [mods and rockers](/wiki/Mods_and_rockers "Mods and rockers"), some mods were seen wearing boots and braces and sporting close cropped haircuts (for practical reasons, as long hair was a liability in industrial jobs and street fights).

Mods and ex-mods were also part of the early [northern soul](/wiki/Northern_soul "Northern soul") scene, a subculture based on obscure 1960s and 1970s American soul records. Some mods evolved into, or merged with, subcultures such as individualists, stylists, and [scooterboys](/wiki/Scooterboy "Scooterboy").[[11]](#cite_note-autogenerated5-11)

### Revivals and later influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mod_(subculture)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Revivals and later influence")]

Main article: [Mod revival](/wiki/Mod_revival "Mod revival")

A [mod revival](/wiki/Mod_revival "Mod revival") started in the late 1970s in the United Kingdom, with thousands of mod revivalists attending scooter rallies in locations such as Scarborough and the Isle of Wight. This revival was partly inspired by the 1979 film *[Quadrophenia](/wiki/Quadrophenia_(film) "Quadrophenia (film)")*, which explores the original 1960s movement, and by mod-influenced bands such as [the Jam](/wiki/The_Jam "The Jam"), [Secret Affair](/wiki/Secret_Affair "Secret Affair"), [the Lambrettas](/wiki/The_Lambrettas "The Lambrettas"), [Purple Hearts](/wiki/Purple_Hearts_(UK_band) "Purple Hearts (UK band)"), [the Specials](/wiki/The_Specials "The Specials") and [the Chords](/wiki/The_Chords_(British_band) "The Chords (British band)"), who drew on the energy of [new wave music](/wiki/New_wave_music "New wave music").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/MODs_in_Box_Hill.jpg/250px-MODs_in_Box_Hill.jpg)](/wiki/File:MODs_in_Box_Hill.jpg)

Mod revivalists at [Box Hill, Surrey](/wiki/Box_Hill,_Surrey "Box Hill, Surrey"), England, in April 2007

The British mod revival was followed by a revival in North America in the early 1980s, particularly in [Southern California](/wiki/Southern_California "Southern California"), led by bands such as [the Untouchables](/wiki/The_Untouchables_(Los_Angeles_band) "The Untouchables (Los Angeles band)").[[7]](#cite_note-disjointed-7)[[8]](#cite_note-socal-8) The mod scene in Los Angeles and [Orange County](/wiki/Orange_County,_California "Orange County, California") was partly influenced by the [2 Tone](/wiki/2_Tone_(music_genre) "2 Tone (music genre)") [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") revival in England, and was unique in its racial diversity, with black, white, Hispanic and Asian participants. The 1990s [Britpop](/wiki/Britpop "Britpop") scene featured noticeable mod influences on bands such as [Oasis](/wiki/Oasis_(band) "Oasis (band)"), [Blur](/wiki/Blur_(band) "Blur (band)"), [Ocean Colour Scene](/wiki/Ocean_Colour_Scene "Ocean Colour Scene") and [the Bluetones](/wiki/The_Bluetones "The Bluetones"). Popular 21st century musicians [Miles Kane](/wiki/Miles_Kane "Miles Kane")[[53]](#cite_note-53) and [Jake Bugg](/wiki/Jake_Bugg "Jake Bugg")[[54]](#cite_note-54) are also followers of the mod subculture.

## Characteristics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mod_(subculture)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Characteristics")]

Dick Hebdige argued that when trying to understand 1960s mod culture, one has to try and "penetrate and decipher the mythology of the mods".[[55]](#cite_note-Hebdige,_Dick_1993._Page_168-55) Terry Rawlings argued that the mod scene developed when British teenagers began to reject the "dull, timid, old-fashioned, and uninspired" British culture around them, with its repressed and class-obsessed mentality and its ["naffness"](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/naff "wikt:naff").[[11]](#cite_note-autogenerated5-11) Mods rejected the "faulty pap" of 1950s pop music and sappy love songs. They aimed at being "cool, neat, sharp, hip, and smart" by embracing "all things sexy and streamlined", especially when they were new, exciting, controversial or modern.[[11]](#cite_note-autogenerated5-11) Hebdige claimed that the mod subculture came about as part of the participants' desire to understand the "mysterious complexity of the metropolis" and to get close to black culture of the Jamaican [rude boy](/wiki/Rude_boy "Rude boy"), because mods felt that black culture "ruled the night hours" and that it had more streetwise "[savoir faire](/wiki/List_of_French_words_and_phrases_used_by_English_speakers "List of French words and phrases used by English speakers")".[[55]](#cite_note-Hebdige,_Dick_1993._Page_168-55) [Shari Benstock](/wiki/Shari_Benstock "Shari Benstock") and Suzanne Ferriss argued that at the "core of the British Mod rebellion was a blatant fetishising of the American consumer culture" that had "eroded the moral fiber of England."[[56]](#cite_note-Fashion_1994-56) In doing so, the mods "mocked the class system that had gotten their fathers nowhere" and created a "rebellion based on consuming pleasures".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The influence of British newspapers on creating the public perception of mods as having a leisure-filled club-going lifestyle can be seen in a 1964 article in *[The Sunday Times](/wiki/The_Sunday_Times "The Sunday Times")*. The paper interviewed a 17-year-old mod who went out clubbing seven nights a week and spent Saturday afternoons shopping for clothes and records. However, few British teens and young adults would have had the time and money to spend this much time going to nightclubs. Paul Jobling and David Crowley argued that most young mods worked 9 to 5 at semi-skilled jobs, which meant that they had much less leisure time and only a modest income to spend during their time off.[[57]](#cite_note-Graphic_Design_1800-57)

### Fashion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mod_(subculture)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Fashion")]

Paul Jobling and David Crowley called the mod subculture a "fashion-obsessed and hedonistic cult of the hyper-cool" young adults who lived in metropolitan London or the new towns of the south. Due to the increasing affluence of post-war Britain, the youths of the early 1960s were one of the first generations that did not have to contribute their money from after-school jobs to the family finances. As mod teens and young adults began using their disposable income to buy stylish clothes, the first youth-targeted boutique clothing stores opened in London in the [Carnaby Street](/wiki/Carnaby_Street "Carnaby Street") and [King's Road](/wiki/King%27s_Road "King's Road") districts.[[58]](#cite_note-58) The streets' names became symbols of, one magazine later stated, "an endless frieze of mini-skirted, booted, fair-haired angular angels".[[59]](#cite_note-seebohm19710719-59) Newspaper accounts from the mid-1960s focused on the mod obsession with clothes, often detailing the prices of the expensive suits worn by young mods, and seeking out extreme cases such as a young mod who claimed that he would "go without food to buy clothes".[[57]](#cite_note-Graphic_Design_1800-57)

Two youth subcultures helped pave the way for mod fashion by breaking new ground: the [beatniks](/wiki/Beatnik "Beatnik"), with their [Bohemian](/wiki/Bohemianism "Bohemianism") image of berets and black turtlenecks, and the [Teddy Boys](/wiki/Teddy_Boy "Teddy Boy"), from whom mod fashion inherited its "narcissistic and fastidious [fashion] tendencies" and the immaculate [dandy](/wiki/Dandy "Dandy") look.[[60]](#cite_note-60) The Teddy Boys paved the way for making male interest in fashion socially acceptable. Prior to the Teddy Boys, male interest in fashion in Britain was often associated with underground homosexuals' subculture and dressing style.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Roundel_of_the_United_Kingdom.svg/250px-Roundel_of_the_United_Kingdom.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Roundel_of_the_United_Kingdom.svg)

[Royal Air Force roundel](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force_roundels "Royal Air Force roundels"), a mod symbol

Jobling and Crowley argued that for working class mods, the subculture's focus on fashion and music was a release from the "humdrum of daily existence" at their jobs.[[57]](#cite_note-Graphic_Design_1800-57) Jobling and Crowley noted that while the subculture had strong elements of [consumerism](/wiki/Consumerism "Consumerism") and shopping, mods were not passive consumers; instead they were very self-conscious and critical, customising "existing styles, symbols and artefacts" such as the [Union Flag](/wiki/Union_Flag "Union Flag") and the Royal Air Force [roundel](/wiki/Roundel "Roundel"), and putting them on their jackets in a [pop art](/wiki/Pop_art "Pop art")-style, and putting their personal signatures on their style.[[10]](#cite_note-Graphic_Design_1800._Page_213-10) Mods adopted new Italian and French styles in part as a reaction to the rural and small-town [rockers](/wiki/Rocker_(subculture) "Rocker (subculture)"), with their 1950s-style leather motorcycle clothes and American [greaser](/wiki/Greaser_(subculture) "Greaser (subculture)") look.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Male mods adopted a smooth, sophisticated look that included tailor-made suits with narrow lapels (sometimes made of [mohair](/wiki/Mohair "Mohair")), [skinny ties](/wiki/Necktie#Skinny_tie "Necktie"), button-down collar shirts, wool or cashmere jumpers (crewneck or V-neck), [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_boot "Chelsea boot") or [Beatle boots](/wiki/Beatle_boots "Beatle boots"), [loafers](/wiki/Loafers "Loafers"), [Clarks](/wiki/C._%26_J._Clark "C. & J. Clark") desert boots, bowling shoes, and hairstyles that imitated the look of French *[Nouvelle Vague](/wiki/French_New_Wave "French New Wave")* film actors.[[61]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-61) A big part of the Mod look was borrowed from the [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League_(clothes) "Ivy League (clothes)") collegiate style from the United States.[[62]](#cite_note-62) A few male mods went against gender norms by using eye shadow, eye-pencil or even lipstick.[[61]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-61) Mods chose scooters over motorbikes partly because they were a symbol of Italian style and because their body panels concealed moving parts and made them less likely to stain clothes with oil or road dust. Many mods wore ex-military [parkas](/wiki/Parka#fishtail_parka "Parka") while driving scooters to keep their clothes clean.

Many female mods dressed androgynously, with short haircuts, men's trousers or shirts, flat shoes, and little makeup – often just pale foundation, brown eye shadow, white or pale lipstick and false eyelashes.[[63]](#cite_note-63) British fashion designer [Mary Quant](/wiki/Mary_Quant "Mary Quant"), who helped popularize the [miniskirt](/wiki/Miniskirt "Miniskirt"), is credited for popularizing mod subculture.[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-korea-65) Miniskirts became progressively shorter between the early and mid-1960s. As female mod fashion became more mainstream, slender models like [Jean Shrimpton](/wiki/Jean_Shrimpton "Jean Shrimpton") and Twiggy began to exemplify the mod look. Maverick fashion designers emerged, such as Quant, who was known for her miniskirt designs, and [John Stephen](/wiki/John_Stephen "John Stephen"), who sold a line named "His Clothes" and whose clients included bands such as Small Faces.[[61]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-61) The television programme *Ready Steady Go!* helped spread awareness of mod fashions to a larger audience. Mod-culture continues to influence fashion, with the ongoing trend for mod-inspired styles such as 3-button suits, Chelsea boots and mini dresses. The Mod Revival of the 1980s and 1990s led to a new era of mod-inspired fashion, driven by bands such as [Madness](/wiki/Madness_(band) "Madness (band)"), [the Specials](/wiki/The_Specials "The Specials") and [Oasis](/wiki/Oasis_(band) "Oasis (band)"). The popularity of the [This Is England](/wiki/This_Is_England_(film_series) "This Is England (film series)") film and TV series also kept mod fashion in the public eye. Today's mod icons include [Miles Kane](/wiki/Miles_Kane "Miles Kane") (frontman of the [Last Shadow Puppets](/wiki/The_Last_Shadow_Puppets "The Last Shadow Puppets")), cyclist [Bradley Wiggins](/wiki/Bradley_Wiggins "Bradley Wiggins") and [Paul Weller](/wiki/Paul_Weller "Paul Weller"), 'The ModFather'.

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mod_(subculture)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Music")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Pete_Townshend_and_Keith_Moon_1967.jpg/250px-Pete_Townshend_and_Keith_Moon_1967.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pete_Townshend_and_Keith_Moon_1967.jpg)

[Pete Townshend](/wiki/Pete_Townshend "Pete Townshend") of [The Who](/wiki/The_Who "The Who") in 1967

The early mods listened to the "sophisticated smoother modern jazz" of musicians such as [Miles Davis](/wiki/Miles_Davis "Miles Davis"), [Charlie Parker](/wiki/Charlie_Parker "Charlie Parker"), [Dave Brubeck](/wiki/Dave_Brubeck "Dave Brubeck") and the [Modern Jazz Quartet](/wiki/Modern_Jazz_Quartet "Modern Jazz Quartet"), as well as the American rhythm and blues (R&B) of artists such as [Bo Diddley](/wiki/Bo_Diddley "Bo Diddley") and [Muddy Waters](/wiki/Muddy_Waters "Muddy Waters"). The music scene of the Mods was a mix of modern jazz, R&B, psychedelic rock and soul.[[66]](#cite_note-66) Terry Rawlings wrote that mods became "dedicated to R&B and their own dances."[[11]](#cite_note-autogenerated5-11) Black American servicemen, stationed in Britain during the early part of the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"), brought over R&B and [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music") records that were unavailable in Britain, and they often sold these to young people in London.[[67]](#cite_note-67) Starting around 1960, mods embraced the off-beat, Jamaican [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") music of artists such as the [Skatalites](/wiki/Skatalites "Skatalites"), [Owen Gray](/wiki/Owen_Gray "Owen Gray"), [Derrick Morgan](/wiki/Derrick_Morgan "Derrick Morgan") and [Prince Buster](/wiki/Prince_Buster "Prince Buster") on record labels such as [Melodisc](/wiki/Melodisc "Melodisc"), [Starlite](/wiki/Starlite "Starlite") and [Bluebeat](/wiki/Blue_Beat_Records "Blue Beat Records").[[68]](#cite_note-68)

The original mods gathered at all-night clubs such as [The Flamingo](/wiki/Flamingo_Club_(London) "Flamingo Club (London)") and [The Marquee](/wiki/Marquee_Club "Marquee Club") in London to hear the latest records and show off their dance moves. As the mod subculture spread across the United Kingdom, other clubs became popular, including [Twisted Wheel Club](/wiki/Twisted_Wheel_Club "Twisted Wheel Club") in Manchester.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

The [British R&B](/wiki/British_rhythm_and_blues "British rhythm and blues")/[rock](/wiki/British_rock_music "British rock music") bands [the Rolling Stones](/wiki/The_Rolling_Stones "The Rolling Stones"), [the Yardbirds](/wiki/The_Yardbirds "The Yardbirds") and [the Kinks](/wiki/The_Kinks "The Kinks") all had mod followings, and other bands emerged that were specifically mod-oriented.[[22]](#cite_note-Bogdanov2002Mod-22) These included The Who, Small Faces, [the Creation](/wiki/The_Creation_(band) "The Creation (band)"), [the Action](/wiki/The_Action "The Action"), [the Smoke](/wiki/The_Smoke "The Smoke") and [John's Children](/wiki/John%27s_Children "John's Children").[[22]](#cite_note-Bogdanov2002Mod-22) The Who's early promotional material tagged them as playing "maximum rhythm and blues", and a name change in 1964 from The Who to The High Numbers was an attempt to cater even more to the mod market. After the commercial failure of the single "[Zoot Suit/I'm the Face](/wiki/Zoot_Suit/I%27m_the_Face "Zoot Suit/I'm the Face")", the band changed its name back to The Who.[[22]](#cite_note-Bogdanov2002Mod-22) Although [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles") dressed like mods for a while (after dressing like rockers earlier), their [beat music](/wiki/Beat_music "Beat music") was not as popular as British R&B among mods.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

### Amphetamines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mod_(subculture)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Amphetamines")]

A notable part of the mod subculture was recreational amphetamine use, which was used to fuel all-night dances at clubs. Newspaper reports described dancers emerging from clubs at 5 a.m. with dilated pupils.[[3]](#cite_note-medicine-3) Some mods consumed a combined amphetamine/barbiturate called [Drinamyl](/wiki/Dexamyl "Dexamyl"), nicknamed "purple hearts".[[71]](#cite_note-71)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Due to this association with amphetamines, Pete Meaden's "clean living" aphorism about the mod subculture may seem contradictory, but the drug was still legal in Britain in the early 1960s, and mods used the drug for [stimulation](/wiki/Stimulation "Stimulation") and [alertness](/wiki/Alertness "Alertness"), which they viewed as different from the [intoxication](/wiki/Substance_intoxication "Substance intoxication") caused by [alcohol](/wiki/Alcohol_(drug) "Alcohol (drug)") and other drugs.[[3]](#cite_note-medicine-3) Andrew Wilson argued that for a significant minority, "amphetamines symbolised the smart, on-the-ball, cool image" and that they sought "stimulation not intoxication ... greater awareness, not escape" and "confidence and articulacy" rather than the "drunken rowdiness of previous generations".[[3]](#cite_note-medicine-3)

Wilson argued that the significance of amphetamines to the mod culture was similar to that of [LSD](/wiki/Lysergic_acid_diethylamide "Lysergic acid diethylamide") and [cannabis](/wiki/Cannabis_(drug) "Cannabis (drug)") within the subsequent [hippie](/wiki/Hippie "Hippie") counterculture. [Dick Hebdige](/wiki/Dick_Hebdige "Dick Hebdige") argued that mods used amphetamines to extend their leisure time into the early hours of the morning and as a way of bridging the gap between their hostile and daunting everyday work lives and the "inner world" of dancing and dressing up in their off-hours.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

### Scooters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mod_(subculture)&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Scooters")]

See also: [Scooter (motorcycle)](/wiki/Scooter_(motorcycle) "Scooter (motorcycle)") and [Vespa](/wiki/Vespa "Vespa")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Vespa_sport.jpg/250px-Vespa_sport.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vespa_sport.jpg)

1963 VBB Standard 150

Many mods drove motor scooters, usually [Vespas](/wiki/Vespa "Vespa") or [Lambrettas](/wiki/Lambretta_(motorscooter) "Lambretta (motorscooter)").[[73]](#cite_note-73) Scooters were a practical and affordable form of transportation for 1960s teens, since until the early 1970s, public transport stopped relatively early in the night. For teens with low-paying jobs, scooters were cheaper and easier to park than cars, and they could be bought through newly available [hire purchase](/wiki/Hire_purchase "Hire purchase") plans.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Mods_Vespa_GT.jpg/250px-Mods_Vespa_GT.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mods_Vespa_GT.jpg)

Vespa with characteristic collection of mirrors

Mods also treated scooters as a [fashion accessory](/wiki/Fashion_accessory "Fashion accessory"). Italian scooters were preferred due to their clean-lined, curving shapes and gleaming [chrome](/wiki/Chrome_plating "Chrome plating"), with sales driven by close associations between dealerships and clubs, such as the [Ace of Herts](/wiki/Ace_of_Herts "Ace of Herts").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

For young mods, Italian scooters were the "embodiment of continental style and a way to escape the working-class row houses of their upbringing".[[74]](#cite_note-straight.com-74) Mods customised their scooters by painting them in "two-tone and [candyflake](/wiki/Candy_Apple_Red "Candy Apple Red") and overaccessorized [them] with luggage racks, crash bars, and scores of mirrors and fog lights".[[74]](#cite_note-straight.com-74) Some mods added four, ten, or as many as 30 mirrors to their scooters. They often put their names on the small windscreen. They sometimes took their engine side panels and front bumpers to electroplating shops to get them covered in highly reflective chrome.

Hard mods (who later evolved into the skinheads) began riding scooters more for practical reasons. Their scooters were either unmodified or [cutdown](/wiki/Cutdown "Cutdown"), which was nicknamed a "skelly".[[75]](#cite_note-75)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Lambrettas were cutdown to the bare frame, and the [unibody](/wiki/Unibody "Unibody") (monocoque)-design Vespas had their body panels slimmed down or reshaped.

After the seaside resort brawls, the media began to associate Italian scooters with violent mods. Much later, writers described groups of mods riding scooters together as a "menacing symbol of group solidarity" that was "converted into a weapon".[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77) With events like the 6 November 1966, "scooter charge" on [Buckingham Palace](/wiki/Buckingham_Palace "Buckingham Palace"), the scooter, along with the mods' short hair and suits, began to be seen as a symbol of subversion.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

### Gender roles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mod_(subculture)&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Gender roles")]

[Stuart Hall](/wiki/Stuart_Hall_(cultural_theorist) "Stuart Hall (cultural theorist)") and Tony Jefferson argued in 1993 that compared to other youth subcultures, the mod scene gave young women high visibility and relative autonomy.[[79]](#cite_note-79) They wrote that this status may have been related both to the attitudes of the mod young men, who accepted the idea that a young woman did not have to be attached to a man, and to the development of new occupations for young women, which gave them an income and made them more independent. Hall and Jefferson noted the increasing number of jobs in boutiques and women's clothing stores, which, while poorly paid and lacking opportunities for advancement, gave young women disposable income, status and a glamorous sense of dressing up and going into town to work.[[80]](#cite_note-Resistance_Through_Rituals_1993-80)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

Hall and Jefferson argued that the presentable image of female mod fashions meant it was easier for young mod women to integrate with the non-subculture aspects of their lives (home, school and work) than for members of other subcultures.[[80]](#cite_note-Resistance_Through_Rituals_1993-80) The emphasis on clothing and a stylised look for women demonstrated the "same fussiness for detail in clothes" as their male mod counterparts.[[80]](#cite_note-Resistance_Through_Rituals_1993-80)

Shari Benstock and Suzanne Ferriss claimed that the emphasis in the mod subculture on consumerism and shopping was the "ultimate affront to male working-class traditions" in the United Kingdom, because in the working-class tradition, shopping was usually done by women.[[56]](#cite_note-Fashion_1994-56) [*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]They argued that British mods were "worshipping leisure and money ... scorning the masculine world of hard work and honest labour" by spending their time listening to music, collecting records, socialising, and dancing at all-night clubs.[[56]](#cite_note-Fashion_1994-56)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

### Conflicts with rockers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mod_(subculture)&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Conflicts with rockers")]

Main article: [Mods and rockers](/wiki/Mods_and_rockers "Mods and rockers")

In early-1960s Britain, the two main [youth subcultures](/wiki/Youth_subculture "Youth subculture") were mods and [rockers](/wiki/Rocker_(subculture) "Rocker (subculture)"). Mods were described in 2012 as "effeminate, stuck-up, emulating the middle classes, aspiring to a competitive sophistication, snobbish, [and] phony", and rockers as "hopelessly naive, loutish, [and] scruffy", emulating the motorcycle gang members in the film *[The Wild One](/wiki/The_Wild_One "The Wild One")*, by wearing leather jackets and riding motorcycles.[[4]](#cite_note-Brawl-4)[[81]](#cite_note-81) [Dick Hebdige](/wiki/Dick_Hebdige "Dick Hebdige") claimed in 2006 that the "mods rejected the rocker's crude conception of masculinity, the transparency of his motivations, his clumsiness"; the rockers viewed the vanity and obsession with clothes of the mods as immasculine.[[14]](#cite_note-DickHebdige-14)

Scholars debate how much contact the two subcultures had during the 1960s. Hebdige argued that mods and rockers had little contact with each other because they tended to come from different regions of England (mods from London and rockers from rural areas), and because they had "totally disparate goals and lifestyles".[[55]](#cite_note-Hebdige,_Dick_1993._Page_168-55) Mark Gilman, however, claimed that both mods and rockers could be seen at [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") matches.[[82]](#cite_note-82)

John Covach wrote that in the United Kingdom, rockers were often engaged in brawls with mods.[[4]](#cite_note-Brawl-4) [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") News stories from May 1964 stated that mods and rockers were jailed after riots in seaside resort towns on the south and east coasts of England, such as [Margate](/wiki/Margate "Margate"), [Brighton](/wiki/Brighton "Brighton"), [Bournemouth](/wiki/Bournemouth "Bournemouth") and [Clacton](/wiki/Clacton-on-Sea "Clacton-on-Sea").[[83]](#cite_note-83) The "mods and rockers" conflict was explored as an instance of "[moral panic](/wiki/Moral_panic "Moral panic")" by sociologist Stanley Cohen in his study *Folk Devils and Moral Panics*,[[5]](#cite_note-Moral_Panics-5) which examined media coverage of the mod and rocker riots in the 1960s.[[6]](#cite_note-British_Film_Commission_BFC-6) Although Cohen acknowledged that mods and rockers had some fights in the mid-1960s, he argued that they were no different from the evening brawls that occurred between non-mod and non-rocker youths throughout the 1950s and early 1960s, both at seaside resorts and after football games.[[84]](#cite_note-Cohen,_Stanley_page_27-84)

Newspapers of the time were eager to describe the mod and rocker clashes as being of "disastrous proportions", and labelled mods and rockers as "sawdust Caesars", "vermin" and "louts".[[5]](#cite_note-Moral_Panics-5) Newspaper editorials fanned the flames of hysteria, such as a *[Birmingham Post](/wiki/Birmingham_Post "Birmingham Post")* editorial in May 1964 which warned that mods and rockers were "[internal enemies](/wiki/Enemy_of_the_state "Enemy of the state")" in the United Kingdom who would "bring about disintegration of a nation's character". The magazine *Police Review* argued that the mods and rockers' purported lack of respect for law and order could cause violence to "surge and flame like a forest fire".[[5]](#cite_note-Moral_Panics-5)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] As a result of this media coverage, two British members of parliament travelled to the seaside areas to survey the damage, and MP [Harold Gurden](/wiki/Harold_Gurden "Harold Gurden") called for a resolution for intensified measures to control youth [hooliganism](/wiki/Hooliganism "Hooliganism"). One of the prosecutors in the trial of some of the Clacton brawlers argued that mods and rockers were youths with no serious views, who lacked respect for law and order.
